title: What An American thinks about Spain

transcript.text: Alright, when I think about Spain, Spain is very hilly, a lot hillier than France. I did Spain from, you know, western side of Spain to Pamplona, to Madrid, to Portugal, to Gibraltar on my bike.
And then I'd take the bus to Monaco from there, kind of stopping in Valencia and Granada or whatever. Well, Granada and Barcelona.
But yeah, so it was like really hilly. And it was like red, like red earth. It was like desert. It was like kind of reminding me of the southwest of America, like deserty red.
Like whereas France and, you know, up north there was like more like forests and grassy fields and stuff.
And, but it wasn't as crowded. It seemed a lot less populated than France and, you know, Holland and England.
Like there were lots of areas where it was like you're in America. It was like you're riding and there's just in the fields and, you know, up and over pass.
And it's just like no houses or anything. But it was really weird because there were like no cars at all.
It was like an abandoned country. Like there'd be like three hours would go by before another car would come.
And I remember riding up some pass and maybe it was on Sunday and on Sundays nobody even leaves the house or something.
But like another weird thing is that like you go into cities and it would be like abandoned, like totally abandoned.
You wouldn't see anybody in the town. And then the sun goes down and everybody comes out and it's like totally bustling all of a sudden.
You know, and then the middle of the day there's like nobody. I think they have a thing called siesta where everybody closes down the, down all of their restaurants and everything.
And they all sleep. They all go inside and like sleep.
As far as the people, I found that, I found that they're not, they don't really speak English and they seem to be hostile to me as an American more, just as much as the French or maybe even more.

Um, like I was in Barcelona and one guy like got like really angry with me because he didn't think I was doing good enough of a job of, of like paying him for a loaf of bread or like I was taking too long.
And he was like, like yelling at me like that, like had never happened even in America or any other country.
And then like three occasions I'd ask some people like where like a hostel was or a street was, and they'd point me in the exact wrong direction.
And I kind of thought, I was in the impression they did that on purpose.
But, um, and the food was weird. Like I didn't find the food to be good.
Um, not like, not a whole lot of, uh, of, of choice.
Um, uh, like I would like lots of salads.
Like, I mean, I guess it was health food, healthy food, but it was kind of bland dish.
It seemed, um, I didn't speak Spanish, so I'd like see ensalada and I was like, oh, cool.
Enchilada. Um, and inch, but ensalada means salad.
So I'd be like, cool. My enchilada. And they give me a salad. Um, and another weird thing about Spain.
Oh yeah. Another actually cool thing about Spain is like the towns are like really small.
It's like going back in time. Like you, you could see a town from a long ways away from a ways away is like, uh,
It's just like totally consolidated, like totally bleached white. Everything's white.
Um, and, um, all the buildings are all together kind of like in like beyond a, behind a wall.
And maybe they even had a wall, you know, like into the gates of the city.
Um, and there's like no suburbs, like no, no trickle effect.
As you go into the city, there's a house and there's more houses and then you're in the city.
Like no Spain, like you're, you see the city, you're right up to it.
There's nothing, nothing, nothing. And then you like cross the threshold and boom, you're in the city.
Um, which I thought was really pretty.
Um, and they're not really a lot of trees either.
So you can see the city totally stand out as you're coming up to it.
It's just like, yeah, a lot of this country was like totally barren of trees, just like little bushes.
And that's like it. Um, um, and another weird thing about the city is in a lot of places,
They didn't really clean up their trash.
Like there'd be like piles of rubble, piles of bricks and cement all over the place.
Like in Granada and a lot of, and some other places, I don't know what their deal was with that.
Maybe. I mean, I know an English guy said that his dad went there in like the seventies and felt like he was in a third world country.
And I kind of had that impression. Like it wasn't a first world country infrastructure wise.
Um, there's just lots of weird stuff about it.
Like just how to be abandoned and, and, and like not any people on any other roads.
Um, yeah, I was kind of boring like that, but yeah, Spanish people, man, like another time I bought a CD player.
And then I, as I walked, as I was walking out, I bought it for like a really expensive, like 130 bucks.
And as I was walking out, I looked in the window and I saw they were selling it for like 20 bucks less.
So they totally ripped me off.
So I turned around and I showed him, I was like, look, and they're trying to pretend that they didn't speak English and French, Spanish don't speak English at all.
They're like the French. They don't speak any English.
And then what I found is like, look, dude, you overcharged me for this.
Is it cause I'm American or something? And he's like, okay, wait, wait.
And I waited for like an hour while he was helping all these other people.
And then like when people, there wasn't anybody staying in line, he pretend like he didn't see me.
And I finally came up to him and I'm like, you ready?
And then he goes, ah, and he gives me like four, four batteries to like make up for it.
And, um, so I was like, all right, whatever.
And I left and, and then that thing broke like a couple of weeks later.
Totally pissed me off.
So, um, I didn't really have a good, and I think they even have like a derogatory slang term for Americans.
Yeah, they, they don't like Americans in Spain.
My experience was, um, and, um, and it seemed like it was cheaper than France.
It seemed like France was maybe a little bit cheaper than, than England.
France and England were both more expensive than America as far as food.
Like eight bucks for a Happy Meal type of a thing.
And, uh, or for, you know, a number one meal deal at the fast food place.
And Spain was a little bit cheaper, like six bucks.
Um, and, and, but there were lots of McDonald's in Spain too that were busy that I went to.
I didn't really have that much of a trouble.
And then I'd like camp out in like the rebel-strewn, like, area.
Um, and then I, I went to like, um, Barcelona and that was pretty, uh,
It was like a big party scene.
There was this, uh, hostel that was like multi-storied.
And then inside the hostel they had a beer vending machine.
So that was cool.
First time I'd seen anything like that.
And then they have lots of like street performers in Barcelona.
Like lots of them, more than anybody had ever seen.
And they have really interesting architecture in Spain too.
Like, but I didn't really like it.
I found it creepy.
It was like gothic.
Like buildings with twisted, twisted forms and gargoyles.
And just like, yeah, I didn't, I didn't like it.
But it was like really artistic.
The most artistic architecture.
Maybe I just didn't think the art, artism was, I don't know,
It was like overdone, like tacky, kind of like a Russian subway or something.
But, um, and the, uh, the street performers I thought were kind of creepy too.
Like, cause they'd like try to get, they'd like hassle you to give them money.
Like wave their thing out.
I don't know.
Spain was one of the countries I wasn't really impressed with.
Maybe I could go back and now that I speak Spanish, but I don't even understand the Spanish.
Like I'm perfect in Spanish.
And when I see a Spanish movie, it's like, what did you say?
They, they lisp.
They've got this lisp and they talk so fast.
I could understand an Argentinian or a Chilean or anybody way easier than a Spanish person.
So, and there, but there are lots of, there's a few bikers in Spain.
I saw one guy I was drafting off of for a while.
So, before I think about Spain.
